author: Richard Hamming
title: You and Your Research
desc: A legendary talk on doing great work through long-term curiosity, problem selection, courage, and independent thinking.

author: Shane Parrish
title: The Map Is Not the Territory
desc: Explains how models, abstractions, and beliefs are simplified representations of reality rather than reality itself.

author: E. M. Forster
title: The Machine Stops
desc: A prophetic 1909 sci-fi story about technological dependence, isolation, algorithmic mediation, and civilizational collapse.

author: Kyle Chayka
title: How the Internet Turned Us Into Content Machines
desc: Examines how online platforms push people to constantly package identity, creativity, and life into monetizable content.

author: Mike Caulfield
title: The Garden and the Stream: A Technopastoral
desc: Contrasts open information streams with carefully curated knowledge gardens as two modes of consuming information online.

author: Scott Alexander
title: Meditations on Moloch
desc: Explores how competition, coordination failure, and incentive systems push societies toward destructive outcomes.

author: Richard Barbrook and Andy Cameron
title: The Californian Ideology
desc: Critiques Silicon Valley’s fusion of libertarianism, techno-utopianism, counterculture, and neoliberal economics.

author: Jo Freeman
title: The Tyranny of Structurelessness
desc: Argues that informal groups without explicit structure often create hidden power hierarchies instead of true equality.

author: Unknown
title: Appreciation Over Aspiration: A $600B Shift
desc: Discusses the cultural and economic movement from aspirational consumption toward appreciation, ownership, and meaning.

author: Neo Kim
title: If you're still thinking like a software engineer...
desc: A short reflection on shifting from pure engineering execution toward systems thinking and business leverage.

author: Polymathic Being
title: How to Research Anything
desc: A practical framework for investigating unfamiliar topics using layered reading, synthesis, and iterative curiosity.

author: Paul Ford
title: What Is Code?
desc: A sprawling interactive essay explaining software, programming culture, and the hidden systems running modern life.

author: Rich Sutton
title: The Bitter Lesson
desc: Argues that scalable computation and general methods consistently outperform handcrafted human-designed AI systems over time.

author: Ashis Nandy
title: Some excerpts from Nandy’s “The Intimate Enemy”
desc: Reflections on colonialism’s psychological effects and how empires reshape identity, masculinity, and self-perception.

name: Definitive List of Things Startups Did That Didn't Work
desc: Crowdsourced archive documenting failed startup strategies, experiments, growth tactics, and business mistakes.

author: IEET
title: Tech Policy To-Do List
desc: Policy-focused essay outlining urgent governance and regulatory challenges created by emerging technologies and AI systems.

author: Strategyzer
title: How To Prototype Radically Different Business Models
desc: Framework for rapidly testing unconventional business models before fully committing resources.

author: Sternberg Press
title: Sternberg Press
desc: Independent publishing house focused on contemporary art, critical theory, architecture, visual culture, and experimental academic writing.

author: Set Margins’ press
title: Set Margins
desc: Experimental publishing platform exploring digital culture, design, internet aesthetics, philosophy, and contemporary media criticism.

author: TIAT
title: the intersection of art & technology
desc: Curated publication and platform documenting projects, essays, and conversations at the boundary of art, computation, and emerging technology.

author: 3 Quarks Daily
title: 3 Quarks Daily
desc: Long-running intellectual magazine aggregating thoughtful writing across science, philosophy, politics, literature, and the arts.

author: Maria Popova
title: The Marginalian
desc: Reflective essays connecting literature, philosophy, science, poetry, and human meaning through deeply researched storytelling.

author: Noema Magazine
title: NOEMA
desc: Magazine focused on the future of technology, geopolitics, consciousness, AI, systems thinking, and civilizational change.

author: Aeon
title: Aeon
desc: Digital magazine publishing accessible longform essays and ideas spanning psychology, philosophy, science, society, and culture.

author: The Pudding
title: The Pudding
desc: Interactive visual essay publication using data storytelling, design, and animation to reveal hidden cultural and behavioral patterns.

author: The Public Domain Review
title: The Public Domain Review
desc: Online journal curating unusual public-domain books, illustrations, archives, maps, manuscripts, and forgotten historical media.

author: Agustín Fuentes
title: I put a camera on a monkey. Here’s how it shook my understanding of humanity
desc: Using monkey point-of-view footage, Fuentes argues that human behavior and sociality are far less unique than we often assume.

author: Luke Brunning and Per-Erik Milam
title: Moral luck ....Two people drive drunk at night: one kills a pedestrian, one doesn’t. Does the unlucky killer deserve more blame or not?
desc: Explores the moral difference between condemning actions versus labeling entire people as inherently bad.

author: Clifford Stott
title: A belief in meritocracy is not only false: it’s bad for you
desc: Argues that meritocracy myths distort social reality, justify inequality, and increase stress, narcissism, and self-blame.

author: Miriam Schoenfield
title: Why do you believe what you do? Run some diagnostics on it
desc: Practical framework for examining whether your beliefs come from evidence, social influence, emotion, or intellectual luck.

author: Henrik Karlsson
title: Everything that turned out well in my life followed the same design process
desc: Personal essay arguing that meaningful work and life paths emerge gradually through experimentation rather than rigid planning.

author: Devon Price
title: Laziness Does Not Exist
desc: Challenges the idea of laziness by arguing that exhaustion, fear, burnout, and structural pressure are usually the real causes behind low productivity.

author: Ayelet Fishbach, Juliano Laran and Ravi Dhar
title: The Elusive Green Consumer
desc: Explains why environmentally conscious consumers often fail to act consistently and how businesses can better align incentives with sustainable behavior.

author: Alex Gaynor and contributors
title: What happens when you type google.com into your browser and press enter?
desc: Deep practical walkthrough of everything happening between typing a URL and loading a webpage, covering DNS, TCP/IP, HTTPS, browsers, rendering, and servers.

author: Adam Wiggins
title: The Twelve-Factor App
desc: Foundational methodology for building scalable cloud-native software applications with clean deployment, configuration, logging, and process management practices.

author: Debt Serious
title: Round #35: Back Leverage: Bank & Private Credit
desc: Explains how modern finance quietly layers leverage through banks, private credit, and shadow financial structures beyond public markets.

author: Tomas Pueyo
title: Why Warm Countries Are Poorer
desc: Argues geography and climate heavily shape economic development through agriculture, disease burden, transportation, institutions, and energy efficiency.

author: Rohit Shinde
title: India doesn't have a right wing
desc: Political commentary arguing that Indian political factions often lack coherent ideological alignment with classical right-wing traditions.

author: Atharva Chaporkar
title: Horn "OK" Please?
desc: Reflective essay using Indian truck slogans and road culture to explore identity, communication, aesthetics, and everyday public life.

author: David McRaney
title: Confirmation Bias
desc: Explains how people selectively notice and interpret information that reinforces existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence.

author: Erik Baker
title: What Are You Going to Do With That?
desc: Critiques modern higher education’s shift from intellectual development toward career optimization and economic signaling.

author: Dan Hendrycks
title: The Politics Of Superintelligence
desc: Examines how advanced AI systems could reshape global power, governance, military competition, and institutional stability.

author: Michele Micheletti
title: A Brief History of Consumer Culture
desc: Historical overview of how industrialization, advertising, globalization, and identity shaped modern consumer behavior.

author: Joe Carlsmith
title: Building AIs that do human-like philosophy
desc: Explores whether AI systems could reason philosophically in ways similar to humans and what that means for alignment and cognition.

author: Jonathan Birch
title: The Mythology Of Conscious AI
desc: Challenges popular assumptions about conscious machines and argues that human narratives often exaggerate AI sentience.

author: Tony Prescott
title: The synthetic psychology of the self
desc: Academic exploration of how robotics, neuroscience, and cognitive modeling can simulate human-like concepts of selfhood and identity.

author: Philip Ball
title: The Trouble with Scientists
desc: Science is distorted by human bias; researchers favor confirmation while journals reward 'neat' results, leading to a slow self-correction process despite improved transparency tools.

author: Paul Graham
title: Cities and Ambition
desc: live where people chase X → you start chasing X; cities quietly set the default definition of success and pull you towards it.

author: PALLADIUM
title: The Past and Future of Military Drones
desc: how drones quietly shifted war from 'protect pilots' to 'scale cheap, autonomous kill systems'—and why the next leap is less about hardware and more about coordination and mass.

author: George Mack
title: High Agency in 30 Minutes
desc: The mindset that separates people who make things happen from people who wait for them to happen. Short, punchy, worth re-reading once a year.

author: Zilan Qian
title: China's AI Optimism Isn't What It Seems
desc: What looks like Chinese enthusiasm for AI actually masks deep economic anxiety rooted in memories of 1990s mass layoffs, reframing it as desperate adaptation rather than genuine optimism.

author: Anthropic
title: Project Glasswing: An initial update
desc: Anthropic's AI-powered security initiative found over 10,000 high-severity vulnerabilities in critical software in its first month, revealing that finding flaws has become trivial while patching remains the bottleneck.

author: Jasmine Sun
title: Party in the Permanent Underclass
desc: Explores why China experiences less visible panic than the US about AI-driven job displacement, arguing that policy intervention is necessary rather than relying on market-led optimism.

author: thariqs
title: The unreasonable effectiveness of HTML — examples
desc: Showcases 20 self-contained HTML files demonstrating that HTML's native interactivity, spatial layout, and rendering make it far more expressive than markdown for AI-generated documents.

author: MindStudio
title: Multi-Agent Reliability Math: Why Chaining 5 Agents Drops Success Rate to 77%
desc: Shows mathematically how chaining five 95%-reliable AI agents produces only 77% system success rate, and outlines mitigations including retry logic and parallel processing.

author: MindStudio
title: What Is the Reliability Compounding Problem in AI Agent Stacks?
desc: Explains how sequential AI agent components multiply failure rates, advocating for shorter critical paths and smart retries to maintain system reliability.
